coredata.dc:title: A photonic dual nano-hybrid assay for detection of cell-free circulating mitochondrial DNA
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Shandilya, Ruchita
Kumari, Roshani
Bunkar, Neha
Bhargava, Arpit
Chaudhury, Koel
Goryacheva, Irina Yu.
Mishra, Pradyumna Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Circulating cell free mitochondrial DNA (ccf-mtDNA) has emerged as a potential marker for diagnosis and prognosis of different chronic and age associated non-communicable diseases. Therefore, owing to its biomarker potential, we herein assessed a novel nano-photonic dual hybrid assay system for rapid and specific detection of ccf-mtDNA. The assay comprised of two systems, i.e. a capture and screen facet containing aminopyrene tethered carbon quantum dots for effective screening of circulating cell free nucleic acids (ccf-NAs) and a quantum dot conjugated probe for precise detection of ccf-mtDNA in the screened ccf-NAs. Our observations suggested that the developed dual-assay system possesses high feasibility and selectivity in screening of ccf-NAs and estimation of ccfmtDNA in a given sample. It also offers high versatility of measurement in different analytical platforms, indicating the translational potential of the method for possible disease risk assessment in control and field settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Circulating nucleic acids
Circulating mitochondrial DNA
Quantum dots
Fluorescence-based assay
Translational research

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of mitochondrial integrity through mtDNA copy number
Fabrication of the “capture and screen” facet of the assay
Fabrication of the “identify and detect” facet of the assay
Assessment of through fluorescence microscopy, fluorometry and flow cytometry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Indian Council of Medical Research, Department of Health Research, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of India , New Delhi (India) for funding support to the laboratory of Professor Pradyumna Kumar Mishra. The technical support received from Mr. Pushpendra Kumar Gupta and Ms. Anju Chouksey is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic representation of the fluorescence based dual assay for the screening of circulating cell-free nucleic acids (ccf-NAs) and accurate identification of ccf-mitochondrial DNA (ccf-mtDNA) in a given sample.

(a) Schematic illustration of fabrication of “capture and screen” facet of the fluorescence based dual-assay. (b) Diagrammatic representation of preparation of the “identify and detect” reporter facet of the fluorescence based dual-assay.

(a) Gel electrophoretic mobility assay of the un-conjugated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (lane 1); un-conjugated 1-aminopyrene (1-AP) (lane 2); and CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid (lane 3) as observed in gel imager. (b) Gel electrophoretic mobility assay of the un-conjugated QD705 (lane 1); un-conjugated ProbeL (lane 2); and QD705-ProbeL conjugate (lane 3) as observed in gel imager.

(a) Figure showing the fluorescence microscopy images demonstrating the CQDs (blue), 1-AP (green), CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid (blue-green) and ccf-NAs captured on CQD-AP confirmed using propidium iodide (blue-green-red). (b) Visual observations of QD705-ProbeL conjugate (red) and ccf-mtDNA QD705-ProbeL nano-composite in fluorescence microscopy wherein the presence of mtDNA in the nano-composite was confirmed using another nucleobases intercalating dye, DAPI (red-blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Graphs illustrating the comparison of the ability of the CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugated systems in screening of ccf-NAs and precise determination of ccf-mtDNA in PBS (enriched in target molecules) with respect to un-conjugated CQDs and QD705, respectively. Bars represent mean± SE values. (b) Graphs depicting the applicability of the CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugate in screening of ccf-NAs and precise determination of ccf-mtDNA in plasma samples with respect to un-conjugated CQDs and QD705, respectively. Bars represent mean± SE values.

(a) Histogram plots demonstrating the shift in fluorescence intensity peak of (mean values, n = 3) (a) CQD on π-stacking of AP and on π-forces based capture of ccf-NAs by the CQD-AP nano-hybrid; (b) QD705 on conjugation probeL by EDC-NHS chemistry and specific identification of mtDNA by the QD705-ProbeL conjugate among the circulating nucleic acids in plasma. (b) Graphs demonstrating the comparison of the ability of the CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugated systems in screening of ccf-NAs and precise determination of ccf-mtDNA in PBS (enriched in target molecules) with respect to un-conjugated CQDs and QD705, respectively in terms of mean fluorescence intensity values (n = 3). Bars represent mean± SE values.(c) Graphs showing the comparison of the ability of the CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugated systems in screening of ccf-NAs and precise determination of ccf-mtDNA in plasma samples with respect to un-conjugated CQDs and QD705, respectively in terms of mean fluorescence intensity values (n = 3). Bars represent mean± SE values.

A representative dot plot showing the flow cytometric analysis of the (a) blank (i.e., plasma); (b) ccf-NAs captured by CQD-AP nano-hybrid in plasma; (c) QD705-ProbeL conjugate identifying mtDNA in plasma; (d) mtDNA precisely identified by QD705-ProbeL conjugate among ccf-NAs captured by CQD-AP nano-hybrid; and (e) An overlay spectra.

head.ce:title: A photonic dual nano-hybrid assay for detection of cell-free circulating mitochondrial DNA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Department of Molecular Biology, ICMR-National Institute for Research in Environmental Health, Bypass Road, Bhauri, Bhopal, MP 462030, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Molecular Biology, ICMR-National Institute for Research in Environmental Health

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Circulating cell free mitochondrial DNA (ccf-mtDNA) has emerged as a potential marker for diagnosis and prognosis of different chronic and age associated non-communicable diseases. Therefore, owing to its biomarker potential, we herein assessed a novel nano-photonic dual hybrid assay system for rapid and specific detection of ccf-mtDNA. The assay comprised of two systems, i.e. a capture and screen facet containing aminopyrene tethered carbon quantum dots for effective screening of circulating cell free nucleic acids (ccf-NAs) and a quantum dot conjugated probe for precise detection of ccf-mtDNA in the screened ccf-NAs. Our observations suggested that the developed dual-assay system possesses high feasibility and selectivity in screening of ccf-NAs and estimation of ccfmtDNA in a given sample. It also offers high versatility of measurement in different analytical platforms, indicating the translational potential of the method for possible disease risk assessment in control and field settings.

ce:keyword.ce:text: aminopyrene tethered carbon quantum dots dual-labelled nano-hybrid

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid nano-technological innovations in the omics-based molecular methodologies have now made it pragmatically possible to precisely identify and decipher diseases-related pathological events at the molecular level [1,2]. In view of this, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), due to their remarkable photo-luminescent attributes, resistance to photo-bleaching and chemical inertness, have emerged as promising nano-labels for therapeutic, diagnostic and prognostic applications [3,4]. One of the characteristic features of CQDs is the carbogenic core surrounded by sp2-hybridized aromatic structures that are stacked together [5]. This structure not only acts as backbone, but also provides CQDs a unique ability of π - π stacking that involves a non-covalent interaction between the aromatic groups by means π - π forces [6]. Such forces are the result of π-orbitals of the aromatic systems of two molecules with rich and deficient electrons. The π stacking leads to delocalization of the π-electrons of the aromatic system that results in the averaging of the electron cloud density, which is highly controlled and stable in comparison to the other non-covalent attachments [7]. Similar to CQDs, pyrene molecule possesses unique fluorescence and colorimetric properties that have significantly attracted researchers for development of highly sensitive and affordable tools [8]. Among the pyrenes, 1-aminopyrene (1-AP) comprising of pyrenyl and amino group have been most commonly used as a linker for immobilization of nucleic acids with π - π stacking interactions on to the carbon nanostructures [9,10]. Interactions that allow intercalation with the nucleobases, in combination with nano-technological frameworks, have opened exciting doors for development of miniaturized sensing platforms for nucleic acid research, molecular diagnostics and material science [8].

Moreover, circulating danger associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) have emerged as a promising biomarker for early disease diagnosis and prognosis. The DAMPs such as lipids, sugars, metabolites, and nucleic acids are often released in the peripheral milieu due to cellular injury. Among these circulating cell-free nucleic acids (ccf-NAs), in particularly, the ccf-mtDNA has attracted significant attention in the recent years as potential disease biomarkers [11,12]. This is mainly because of the vital role of mitochondria in maintaining cellular homeostasis. Cellular insult often result in compromising of the mitochondria that lead to increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS), abnormal regulation of calcium handling, and loss of mitochondrial integrity [13–15]. The disrupted mitochondria are often packaged in lysosomes for removal via the process of mitophagy, however, if considerable amounts of mtDNA are damaged, apoptosis may predominate [16,17]. Accumulation of oxidized mtDNA fragments in the cytosolic compartment escape the imbibition in autophagasomes and may lead to instigation of signalling responses in other cellular compartments (retrograde signalling). The increase in levels of oxidized mtDNA fragments in the cytosol often release free or encapsulated form of mtDNA fragments in the extracellular environment [18,19]. Furthermore, the ccf-mtDNA in the extracellular matrix infiltrate within the neighbouring cells via endocytosis or through binding to receptors for advanced glycated end-products (RAGEs). Such infiltration leads to macrophage activation and TLR9 dependent inflammation, which encourage the tissue and cellular injury along with increase in mitochondrial damage and the loop of reaction repeats [20,21]. Accumulating evidences has reported the association of elevated levels of fragments of ccf-mtDNA with asymptomatic atherosclerosis, acute coronary syndrome, myocardial infarction, pulmonary thromboembolism and heart failure [22–24]. Owing to the diagnostic potential, analysis of the circulating levels of NAs, particularly, ccf-mtDNA might offer a milestone approach for prognosis and diagnosis of cardiovascular risk. Therefore, in the present work we illustrate a novel fluorescence-based dual-assay methodology comprising of aminopyrene tethered CQD (CQD-AP) dual-labelled nano-hybrid structure as “capture and screen” facet for the effective screening of the ccf-NAs based on π-π interactions between nucleic acids and nanomaterials. The second system comprising of quantum dots (QD)-probe conjugates served as an “identify and detect” reporter facet for precise determination of mtDNA among the ccf-NAs, highlighting the pragmatic and versatile nature of the approach for cardiovascular risk assessment ( Fig. 1).

All the reagents used were of the highest available grade. CQDs and 1-AP were purchased from Merck Sigma-Aldrich, KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany. QIAamp Circulating Nucleic Acid isolation Kit for ccf-NAs isolation was procured from Qiagen, Hilden, Germany. Taq 2X master mix was obtained from New England Biolabs (Ipswich, Massachusetts, USA). QD705, EDC (1-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride) and NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide) were procured from Thermofisher Scientific (Waltham, Massachusetts, USA). The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) primers i.e. forward primer 5′-TAGCCATGCACTACTCACCAGA-3′ and reverse primer 5′-GGATGAGGCAGGAATCAAAGAC-3′ of long fragment of mtDNA were procured from Integrated DNA Technologies (Coralville, Iowa, USA). An oligonucleotide sequence 5′-ACAGCGTCACAGACAGAAACTAAGGACGGGGTAGG-3′-amine group customized for precise detection of the long fragment of mtDNA D-loop region (ProbeL) was purchased from Integrated DNA Technologies (Coralville, Iowa, USA) for preparation of the detector element of the assay.

Plasma samples collected from healthy volunteers were subjected to ccf-NAs extraction as per the prescribed instructions of the manufacturer. Briefly, 20 μl proteinase K was added to the 200 μl plasma and vortexed to dissolve the pellet completely. The mixture was incubated at 56 °C for 15 min for lysis and was added to the silica based columns for elution. The eluted ccf-NAs were stored at − 80 °C for further use [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of mitochondrial integrity through mtDNA copy number
ce:section.ce:para: For the mitochondrial integrity assessment, relative quantification of mtDNA copy number was evaluated in a set of 50 samples using quantitative real-time PCR (Insta Q96, Himedia Laboratories, Mumbai, India). The data was expressed as the ratio of the mean mtDNA value to the mean nDNA value (β-actin) of the triplicate measurements for a given sample (mtDNA/ β-actin) [11].

For the characterization of mtDNA in circulation, 40 ng of ccf-DNA isolated from the aforementioned protocol were ligated with 2.5 μl of forward and reverse primers (10 pmole) and 10 μl of Taq 2X Master Mix. The reaction mixture was subjected to amplification (40 cycles) using conventional PCR method in GeneAmp® PCR system 9700 (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, California, USA), at 95 °C for 30 s, 60 °C for 30 s and 68 °C for 1 min 45 s. Following amplification, the obtained PCR products were analysed through electrophoresis on a 0.8% agarose gel and imaged using Gel Doc™ XR + molecular imager (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Hercules, California, USA) [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the “capture and screen” facet of the assay
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to preparation of the screening facet of the assay, 1 mM solution of CQDs in water (1000 μl) and 1 mM solution of 1-AP in ethanol (1000 μl) were prepared. The 1 mM solution of both CQDs and 1-AP were mixed and vortexed for 90 min at room temperature following which the mixture was subjected to overnight incubation at 4 °C. After overnight incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 x g for 10 min and the supernatant was stored at 4 °C. The prepared nano-hybrid was subjected to characterization using gel electrophoretic mobility assay and fluorescence microscopy. The dual-labelled nano-hybrid structure of CQD and 1-AP serves as the unit for effective capture of the ccf-NA in a given sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the “identify and detect” facet of the assay
ce:section.ce:para: For the precise identification of mtDNA, oligonucleotide sequences complementary to the long fragment (ProbeL) of the D-loop region of mtDNA were conjugated with QD705 exhibiting far red fluorescence, using carbodiimide coupling chemistry. For the conjugation, QD705 particles comprising of surface carboxylic groups were attached with 3′-amine modified ProbeL sequences. Prior to reaction, 50 μl solution of QD705 (1 μM) was added with 100 μl of EDC (1 mM) and 100 μl of NHS (1 mM) in a ratio of 1: 1000: 1000 for activation of the carboxylic acid functional groups. The mixture was then incubated for 15 min incubation in ice-cold conditions. Subsequently, 10 μl (100 pM) of 3′ end amine-modified ProbeL was added to the above mixture followed by incubation for 30 min with intermittent vortexing. The mixture was left for overnight incubation and the prepared QD705-ProbeL conjugates were appropriately evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of through fluorescence microscopy, fluorometry and flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of the developed dual-assay for screening of ccf-NAs and precise identification of ccf-mtDNA among the circulating NAs (present in plasma) was assessed by fluorescence microscopy and determined using fluorometry and flow cytometry.

For fluorescence microscopy, 1–2 drops of each sample of CQDs; 1-AP; CQD-AP nano-hybrid without ccf-NAs; CQD-AP with ccf-NAs and PI; QD705-ProbeL conjugates; and CQD-AP bound ccf-mtDNA with QD705-ProbeL conjugates were applied on clean glass slides followed by cover-slip application. The prepared slides were analysed in the fluorescence microscopy with appropriate filters.

ccf-NAs in the samples (PBS or 0.45μ filtered plasma) were screened using fluorometry. For the measurement, 10 μl of un-conjugated CQDs and CQD-AP suspended in 1X PBS were analysed as negative controls with respect to 1X PBS in the 380 nm excitation and 500 nm emission wavelength. This was followed by analysis of CQD-AP with ccf-NAs (10 μl) re-suspended in 1X PBS, resulting in generation of a CQD-AP: ccf-NAs nano-composite, that was investigated for the determination of fluorescence intensity measurements. ccf-mtDNA among the circulating nucleic acids were precisely identified by the QD-ProbeL conjugates in 1X PBS samples and compared. Samples comprising of CQD-AP nanohybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugates with mtDNA enriched samples (in PBS) were analysed as positive controls. Further, the prepared nano-conjugates were analyzed for the applicability of the assay in real-time detection in intricate biological samples. For the analysis, 0.45μ filtered plasma without the addition of the nano-conjugates served as blank. CQD-AP with 10 μl each ccf-NAs spiked in 0.45 μ filtered plasma were analysed fluorescence intensity measurements resulting from the CQD-AP: ccf-NAs nano-composite. ccf-mtDNA was accurately identified by the QD-ProbeL conjugates in plasma samples in the 485 nm excitation and 705 nm emission wavelength and compared for assessment of the applicability of the assay.

The confirmation of fluorometry results was done by flow cytometry (Attune NXT, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). In brief, 300 μl of 1X PBS and plasma were added with 10 μl of the prepared nano-conjugates and analysed. For the performance assessment, the CQD-AP: ccf-NAs nano-composite and ccf-mtDNA: QD705-ProbeL nano-composite were analysed using appropriate gating and voltage settings in respective channels i.e. VL1-H (excitation laser 405 nm and emission filter 440/50 nm) and YL3-H (excitation laser 561 nm and emission filter 695/40 nm). To identify ccf-mtDNA, CQD-AP and QD705-ProbeL conjugates in plasma and PBS were analysed in the blue positive and far-red positive quadrants respectively while the detection of mtDNA among the ccf-NAs were analysed in the dual-positive quadrant.

Further, sensitivity assessment and determination of quantitative validation parameters such as limit of detection and limit of quantitation was performed using fluorometric method. For the measurement, an increasing concentration of DNA samples ranging from 1 pg to 100,000 pg were spiked in PBS and observed in fluorometry followed by preparation of the calibration curve. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated based on the following formula.
LOD = 3σ / K
LOQ = 10σ / K
where, σ = standard deviation of the blank;.
K = slope of the calibration curve.

Circulating NAs, in particularly, the ccf-mtDNA has gained significant attention as potential biomarkers for disease diagnosis and prognosis. This ability of circulating NAs is attributed to the fact that these biomolecules circulate at differential levels in the normal and diseased states. In fact, the applicability of ccf-mtDNA as predictive biomarker has been assessed in different clinical conditions including cancer and cardiovascular disorders. It has been also shown that the altered levels of mtDNA copy number are closely associated with the pathophysiological events in atherosclerotic cardiovascular disorders. In present study, the mtDNA copy number quantified as a ratio of DNA between the mitochondrial gene and a reference nuclear gene in 50 individual samples showed differential circulating levels. This quantitative measurement provides an insight of disrupted mitochondrial function and mito-nuclear cross talk conferring to the increased mtDNA damage in the given samples. Further characterization of circulating mitochondrial genome showed the presence of long fragment of the D-Loop region of mtDNA. D-loop region of mtDNA is a non-coding triple-helical region that plays a significant role in mtDNA organization. Such sequential analysis of the D-loop region of the mtDNA has been meticulously used for medical diagnostic applications [26,27].

Keeping the diagnostic potential of ccf-NAs, in particularly ccf-mtDNA, we further developed and analysed the ability of the novel fluorescence-based dual-assay system to screen ccf-NAs and detect mtDNA in circulation. The “capture and screening” facet of the assay is comprised of a nano-hybrid in which 1-AP is attached to CQDs via π-π forces. This attachment involves strong intra-molecular non-covalent interactions between the aromatic structures present in the 1-AP molecule and CQD that provides irreversible and stable anchoring on the carbon surface. In addition, the fluorescence properties of CQD-AP and its ability to allow π-based stacking further assist effective capture of ccf-NAs without involving any intricate hybridization or amplification based procedures. We performed the attachment reaction of 1-AP molecule and CQD at physiologic pH (pH 7.4) [28]. Thus, formed dual-labelled nano-hybrid possesses great potential for molecular diagnostics and therapeutics owing to the configurational characteristics of the pyrenyl group and the nucleobases in the NAs backbone structure ( Fig. 2a). The capture and screen facet was further accompanied by the “identify and detect” reporter facet that consisted of a fluorescent nano-crystal QD705. The QD705 with carboxylic acid groups at the surface was conjugated with an oligonucleotide probe complementary to the long fragment of mtDNA D-loop region (QD705-ProbeL conjugate). The conjugation of QD705 with the probe was done by using EDC-NHS coupling chemistry (Fig. 2b) [29].

We confirmed the π-based stacking of the 1-AP with CQDs in the prepared nano-hybrid using agarose gel electrophoresis that demonstrated restricted mobility of the CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid. The mobility of AP was also restricted, which demonstrated that the attachment of 1-AP led to reduction of the negative charges on the surface of CQDs [ Fig. 3(a)]. Likewise, the evaluation of the prepared QD705-ProbeL conjugate by gel electrophoretic mobility assay demonstrated a rapid movement of QD-probe conjugates [Fig. 3(b)]. The attachment with oligonucleotides caused an increase in the surface charge of QDs, thereby resulting in the rapid movement of the conjugates, while the un-reacted probes were observed as a faint band at the bottom of the gel [30,31]. We further calculated the quantum yield (QY) for both aminopyrene tethered CQD and QD705 according to the method discussed in the earlier report [32]. The calculated QY of the aminopyrene tethered CQD was found to be 7.09 while for QD705 it was 0.379.

The prepared screening and reporter facets were further assessed for their detection capability and versatility using fluorescence microscopy, fluorometry and flow cytometry. We observed that ccf-NAs were effectively captured by the self-assembly of nucleobases via π-based stacking owing to strong π- π attractive forces. The stacking of ccf-NAs on the surface of CQDs was confirmed by a nucleobase intercalating fluorogenic dye, propidium iodide (PI) after the formation of CQD-AP: ccf-NAs dual-colored nano-composite structure. The fluorescence microscopy images in the Fig. 4(a) evidently shows the dual-color properties of the CQD-AP: ccf-NAs nano-composite structure. Importantly, the reporter facet of our dual hybrid assay specifically hybridized with the ccf-mtDNA. This led to the generation of a ccf-mtDNA: QD705-ProbeL nano-composite framework, which was noticeably detected in the fluorescence microscopy. For further confirmation, DAPI, another nucleobase intercalating dye was added to the above nano-composite that designated the presence of blue colour (of DAPI) around the red color (of QD705-ProbeL conjugate) indicating the precise detection of mtDNA [Fig. 4(b)].

We further assessed the detection capability using fluorometry, which showed that attachment of ccf-NAs results in an increase in the fluorescence intensity (RFU units) of CQD-AP, when compared with the unconjugated CQDs (λex=380 nm; λem=500 nm). The applicability of the dual-labelled (CQD-AP) nanohybrid in screening of ccf-NAs was further confirmed in the plasma samples, which showed a similar increase in the fluorescence intensity of CQD-AP nano-conjugate in comparison to CQD alone ( Fig. 5a). The resulting change in the fluorescence intensity may be attributed to the increase in the delocalization region, reduction in the bandgap on π-based connection between 1-AP and CQDs, and stacking of nucleic acids, resulting in the red-shifting of the fluorescence intensity [33,34]. We also observed an increase in the fluorescence intensity of QD705-ProbeL conjugates (detector facet) on identification of mtDNA in PBS and plasma samples (Fig. 5b) (λex= 485 nm; λem= 705 nm). One probable reason for this may be the quantum confinement effect resulting from the change in the chemical environment of the QDs upon attachment of ccf-mtDNA [35]. The change in fluorescence intensity of both the nano-conjugates observed in PBS and plasma samples in comparison to the blank and unconjugated fluorescence nanoparticles (CQD and QD705 alone) warranted the applicability of the dual-assay for the real-time recognition of ccf-mtDNA in isolated as well as intricate clinical samples.

We further validated the screening and reporter facet of the dual-assay using flow cytometry. Our results indicated that attachment of ccf-NAs and ccf-mtDNA to CQD-AP dual-labelled nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugates respectively results in the shift of the fluorescence intensity peak ( Fig. 6a). As per the flow cytometry observations presented in the Figs. 6b and 6c, an increase in the fluorescence intensity (%) of both the systems (CQD-AP nano-hybrid and QD705-ProbeL conjugate) was observed in PBS (spiked with ccf-NAs) and plasma samples. A distinct population of mtDNA captured between the CQD-AP and QD705-ProbeL conjugate was noted in the dual-positive quadrant with respect to CQD-AP positive and QD705-ProbeL positive quadrants ( Fig. 7). This conferred the applicability of the “capture and screen” as well as “identify and quantify” facet of the dual-assay for screening of nucleic acids including mtDNA in circulation.

Moreover, the quantitative validation was performed for the determination of the concentration dependence of the signal (or calibration) and further in calculation (Fig. S2 and S3). LOD and LOQ of ccf-NAs using CQD-AP was found to be 2.5 ng and 8 ng respectively. The ccf-mtDNA spiked in PBS samples reported 0.91 ng and 3 ng as LOD and LOQ, respectively. The conjugates exhibited less photobleaching effect conferring the stability of the conjugates and excellent repeatability as shown from the standard error in the flow cytometry and fluorometry data. The assay could precisely determine the mtDNA in the range of 1 pg to 1 ng and 10 pg to 10 ng of ccf-NAs.

Results of the three analytical methods obtained so far, convene the high selectivity and applicability as well as versatility of the dual-assay for real-time quantitative identification of ccf-mtDNA. However, the fluorescence-based dual-assay is applicable for about 30 min since photo-bleaching effect of both the facets on long-term analysis might result in compromising of the analytical measurements. The fluorescence-based dual-assay is capable of providing deep insightful information through determining the differential levels ccf-mtDNA in the normal and diseased states that might further assist in subsequent analysis of the altered biological processes for early diagnosis and prognosis of cardiovascular diseases.

Owing to the central role of mitochondria in various cellular maintenance and apoptotic processes, exposure to various external or internal stimuli often results in compromising of mitochondrial function leading to damage. This results in accumulation of damaged mtDNA in the cytoplasmic compartment, stimulating its escape in the extracellular environment. mtDNA in the extracellular matrix often behave as DAMPs and act as a biomarker for early disease diagnosis and prognosis. We herein designed, a novel nano-photonic point-of-care approach using 1-aminopyrene-tethered CQDs for the screening of ccf-NAs and accurate identification of mtDNA. The unique properties of aminopyrene-CQDs complex allow π- stacking of nulceobases in the backbone structure of ccf-NAs while QD705-ProbeL conjugates offers precise identification of ccf-mtDNA. The assay offers a novel simplified method for screening and detection without the use of any additional steps of PCR amplification. The developed assay exhibits high feasibility in screening of ccf-NAs and exquisite selectivity in estimation of the ccf-mtDNA in a given sample, highlighting the translational potential of the method for human bio monitoring.

Pradyumna Kumar Mishra: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Ruchita Shandilya: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Roshani Kumari: Investigation, Visualization. Neha Bunkar: Investigation, Visualization. Arpit Bhargava: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Koel Chaudhury: Writing – review & editing. Irina Yu. Goryacheva: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Indian Council of Medical Research, Department of Health Research, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of India, New Delhi (India) for funding support to the laboratory of Professor Pradyumna Kumar Mishra. The technical support received from Mr. Pushpendra Kumar Gupta and Ms. Anju Chouksey is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2021.114441.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanophotonic biosensors as point-of-care tools for preventive health interventions
